original_abstract:
Transatlantic data transfers for health research have become increasingly fraught due to the friction between the EU’s stringent General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and US mass surveillance frameworks. A primary concern remains the broad access granted to US law enforcement and national security agencies over personal data originating in the EU. Since the GDPR took effect, a growing body of evidence shows that critical scientific collaborations are being stalled or abandoned, with public institutions in the US facing the most significant hurdles. 

The adoption of a new EU-US Data Privacy Framework in July 2023 offers little hope for a permanent fix, as it faces the same legal vulnerabilities that led the Court of Justice of the EU to invalidate previous agreements. With the research community urgently seeking stable alternatives, the proposed European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation may offer a new pathway to reconciliation. This paper provides a critical analysis of the current legal landscape and proposes a viable strategy for facilitating data flow between public bodies through the specialized mechanisms of the EHDS.
humanized_abstract:
Transatlantic data transfer for health research has become an increasingly contentious issue in the wake of high-profile disagreements over GDPR and US surveillance practices. At the core of the debate is the ongoing divide between the EU’s robust GDPR and the US surveillance apparatus that offers law enforcement and national security agencies almost unfettered access to the data of non-US individuals, including those in the EU. Since the GDPR came into force there have been numerous reports of data sharing agreements being suspended or science collaborations being dropped in part due to constraints on US public institutions.

The new EU-US Data Privacy Framework was adopted in July 2023 and there is little to suggest that it offers a permanent solution to the issues at play. The framework addresses the same issues of law that were considered unlawful by the Court of Justice of the EU in its decisions related to the earlier EU-US Privacy Shield and GDPR validity. In the meantime, the research community continues to seek an effective and permanent solution for the exchange of personal data. A possible way to achieve the needed reconciliation, in the short term at least, is through the European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation that is currently under discussion. In this paper, we provide a detailed analysis of the current regulation of transborder data flows and, through a careful examination of the possible structures of the EHDS and its application to public sector data flows, we propose a possible way of enabling such flows through specific legislative provisions as provided for in the framework of the EHDS.
